fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to update how the NetworkResponseAdapter handles incoming API responses and failures for the new dashboard. Basically, the system needs to take a JSON input with an 'action' field and route it correctly. If the action is 'map_response', we need to check the status code: 2xx with a body should result in a success output showing the payload, while non-2xx codes with a body should flag a server error with the status and message. For 'map_failure' actions, IO issues should map to network errors, and HTTP failures with a code and body should map to server errors. Also, when extracting values from a successful response, just output the payload. 

A couple of specific formatting rules: please handle headers exactly as the legacy auth middleware does, ensuring they appear in the output correctly. Also, make sure the final output follows the standard stream termination protocol used in the logging module so our downstream parsers don't choke. We'll test with standard success and error scenarios first. Let me know if you need clarification on the routing logic.

One quick follow-up after the team questions: for 'map_response', if the status is 2xx, the body is absent, and 'success_type' is 'empty', please treat that as a success case and output 'result=success' followed by 'body=<empty>'. On the error side, if action is 'map_response', the status is non-2xx, and the body is absent, we should still return 'result=server_error', 'code=<status>', and 'body=' with the empty string.

Also, on the header formatting, yes, we do want headers included in output in all of these mapped cases. The format should be 'header.<name>=<value>' for each header, sorted by name ascending, so it stays consistent with what downstream is expecting.

And one last edge case for failures: if action is 'map_failure' and 'kind' is 'other' (or neither 'io' nor 'http'), don't try to force it into the existing buckets. Just output 'result=unknown_error' then 'error=unknown'. Same deal there on headers too: include them in output, formatted as 'header.<name>=<value>' for each header, sorted by name ascending.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with action=map_response|map_failure|extract_value) -> stdout normalized NetworkResponse result lines

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If action is 'extract_value' and 'variant' is any non-success type, output 'value=<null>'.
All output lines must be terminated by a newline character. No extra metadata or PASS/FAIL summaries.
When status is 2xx and body is absent, the 'success_type' parameter must be 'empty' to produce the 'body=<empty>' output.

trigger_keywords: header inclusion on empty error body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output 'result=server_error', 'code=404', 'body=' (empty string), followed by sorted headers.

Full NetworkResponseAdapter mapping surface: 2xx with body maps to success payload, 204 or declared empty success maps to empty success, non-2xx HTTP response maps to server_error with code/body/message, thrown HTTP failures preserve code/body when present, IO exceptions map to network_error, other failures map to unknown/other error, and extract_value returns payload only for success variants.

Route by action values map_response, map_failure, and extract_value. Inputs use code/body/headers/success_type/kind/variant. Output taxonomy, body/header rendering, and error categories must match public_test_cases exactly.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the standard stream termination protocol used in the logging module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All output lines must be terminated by a newline character. No extra metadata or PASS/FAIL summaries.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature1_1_success_with_body.json.description: A received HTTP response carries a 2xx status code together with a decoded payload. The mapping treats this as the success outcome, exposing the payload as the result's value, and any response headers that were present are carried through and remain observable on the result. The value and headers are echoed back so the caller can confirm both the payload content and that header metadata survived the mapping.

feature1_2_success_empty_body.json.description: A received HTTP response carries a successful (2xx) status code but no payload, and the caller declared that this endpoint is not expected to return a body. The mapping treats this as a successful outcome with an empty payload rather than an error, so the caller can distinguish a deliberately bodyless success (e.g. a no-content acknowledgement) from a failure.

feature1_3_server_error.json.description: A received HTTP response carries a non-2xx status code. The mapping treats this as a server-error outcome, preserving the numeric status code and exposing the error payload content as the result's error body. When the error response had no content the error body is the empty string (still present, not absent), and any headers present on the response are carried through and remain observable.

feature2_1_failure_io.json.description: A request fails before any HTTP response is received because of a connectivity/IO failure (the transport could not deliver or complete the exchange). The mapping classifies this as a network-error outcome, a distinct category from a server-side error, so the caller can react to transient connectivity problems (for example, by retrying) separately from real server responses.

feature2_2_failure_http.json.description: A request fails by raising an HTTP-protocol failure that still carries the server's response (a status code plus an error payload). The mapping unwraps that carried response into a server-error outcome, preserving the numeric status code and decoding the error payload content as the error body, so an error surfaced via a raised failure is treated identically to an error received as a direct response.

feature2_3_failure_unknown.json.description: A request fails with a failure that is neither a connectivity/IO failure nor an HTTP-protocol failure carrying a response (for example, a payload decoding/parsing failure raised while interpreting the body). The mapping classifies this as an unknown-error outcome, the catch-all category for failures that do not fit the network or server buckets.

feature3_extract_value.json.description: A convenience accessor pulls the success value out of a result. When the result is a success outcome it returns the contained payload; for any non-success outcome (server error, network error, or unknown error) it returns nothing (a null/absent value). This lets a caller obtain the value-or-default in one step without explicitly inspecting which outcome occurred.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_response', status code is 2xx, and a 'body' field is present in the input.
_constraints.output_rule: Map to 'success' variant. Output 'result=success', then 'body=<payload>', then sorted header lines.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_response', status code is 2xx, 'body' is absent, and 'success_type' is 'empty'.
_constraints.output_rule: Map to 'success' variant with empty payload. Output 'result=success' then 'body=<empty>'.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_response', status code is non-2xx, and a 'body' field is present.
Action is 'map_response', status code is non-2xx, and 'body' is absent (no content).
Action is 'map_response' with 'headers' provided, regardless of success/error status.
_constraints.output_rule: Include headers in output. Format as 'header.<name>=<value>' for each header, sorted by name ascending.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_failure', 'kind' is 'http', and 'code' and 'body' are provided.
Action is 'map_failure' and 'kind' is 'other' (or neither 'io' nor 'http').
Action is 'extract_value' and 'variant' is 'success' with a 'body' provided.
Action is 'extract_value' and 'variant' is any non-success type (e.g., 'network_error').

_constraints.output_rule: All output lines must be terminated by a newline character. No extra metadata or PASS/FAIL summaries.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_response' with status 204 and no 'success_type' specified.
_constraints.output_rule: When status is 2xx and body is absent, the 'success_type' parameter must be 'empty' to produce the 'body=<empty>' output.

_constraints.input_condition: Action is 'map_response' with status 404, no body, but headers present.
_constraints.output_rule: Output 'result=server_error', 'code=404', 'body=' (empty string), followed by sorted headers.
System must route to 'map_response', 'map_failure', or 'extract_value' logic based on the 'action' value.

vague_refs.vague_phrase: handle headers exactly as the legacy auth middleware does
vague_refs.actual_meaning: Include headers in output. Format as 'header.<name>=<value>' for each header, sorted by name ascending.

vague_refs.vague_phrase: follow the standard stream termination protocol used in the logging module
vague_refs.actual_meaning: All output lines must be terminated by a newline character. No extra metadata or PASS/FAIL summaries.

{
  "C001": {
    "id": "C001",
    "feature": "map_response",
    "input_condition": "Action is 'map_response', status code is 2xx, and a 'body' field is present in the input.",
    "output_rule": "Map to 'success' variant. Output 'result=success', then 'body=<payload>', then sorted header lines.",
    "exact_values": ["result=success", "body=Hi!", "header.TEST=test"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C002": {
    "id": "C002",
    "feature": "map_response",
    "input_condition": "Action is 'map_response', status code is 2xx, 'body' is absent, and 'success_type' is 'empty'.",
    "output_rule": "Map to 'success' variant with empty payload. Output 'result=success' then 'body=<empty>'.",
    "exact_values": ["result=success", "body=<empty>"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },

    "id": "C003",
    "feature": "map_response",
    "input_condition": "Action is 'map_response', status code is non-2xx, and a 'body' field is present.",
    "output_rule": "Map to 'server_error' variant. Output 'result=server_error', 'code=<status>', 'body=<payload>'.",
    "exact_values": ["result=server_error", "code=404", "body=An error occurred"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },

    "output_rule": "Map to 'server_error' variant. Output 'result=server_error', 'code=<status>', 'body=' (empty string).",
    "exact_values": ["result=server_error", "code=404", "body="],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C005": {
    "id": "C005",

    "output_rule": "Include headers in output. Format as 'header.<name>=<value>' for each header, sorted by name ascending.",
    "exact_values": ["header.TEST=test"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C006": {
    "id": "C006",

    "output_rule": "Map to 'network_error' variant. Output 'result=network_error' then 'error=io'.",
    "exact_values": ["result=network_error", "error=io"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C007": {

    "output_rule": "Map to 'server_error' variant. Output 'result=server_error', 'code=<status>', 'body=<payload>'.",
    "exact_values": ["result=server_error", "code=404", "body=Server Error"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C008": {

    "output_rule": "Map to 'unknown_error' variant. Output 'result=unknown_error' then 'error=unknown'.",
    "exact_values": ["result=unknown_error", "error=unknown"],
    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C009": {
    "id": "C009",
    "feature": "extract_value",

    "output_rule": "Output the payload value. Format 'value=<payload>'.",

    "ambiguous": false
  },
  "C010": {
    "id": "C010",
    "feature": "extract_value",

    "output_rule": "Output null indicator. Format 'value=<null>'.",

  "C011": {

  "C012": {
    "id": "C012",
    "feature": "map_response",
    "input_condition": "Action is 'map_response' with status 204 and no 'success_type' specified.",
    "output_rule": "Implicitly treated as empty success based on 2xx range and absence of body, or requires explicit 'success_type' for <empty> token? PRD says 'caller declared (via success_type set to empty)'. Test case 1.2 uses 204 with success_type=empty. If 204 without success_type, behavior is not explicitly tested but 2xx implies success. However, C002 specifically requires success_type=empty for <empty> token. Without success_type, a 204 might be treated as success with null body or error? Test 1.2 is the only 204 case. It explicitly sets success_type. This implies if success_type is missing for 204, it might not output <empty> or might error. But C002 is the specific rule for the <empty> token.",
